lineage: Life and Works of Rufinus with Jerome's Apology Against Rufinus.
Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.

My ignorance of many natural phenomena is no excuse for your ignorance as to the origin of souls. You ought, according to your boasting dream to know everything. The thing of most importance was forgotten in your cargo of Eastern wares.

title: My ignorance of many natural phenomena is no excuse for your ignorance as to the origin of souls. You ought, according to your boasting dream to know everything. The thing of most importance was forgotten in your cargo of Eastern wares.

source.title: NPNF2-03. Theodoret, Jerome, Gennadius, & Rufinus: Historical Writings

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You pass on to the origin of souls, and at great length exclaim against the smoke which you say I raise.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You want to be allowed to express ignorance on a point on which you advisedly dissemble your knowledge; and therefore begin questioning me about angels and archangels; as to the mode of their existence, the place and nature of their abodes, the differences, if there be any, existing between them; and then as to the course of the sun, the waxing and waning of the moon, the character and movements of the stars.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I wonder that you did not set down the whole of the lines:

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Whence come the earthquakes, whence the high-swoll’n seas

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Breaking their bounds, then sinking back to rest;

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The Sun’s eclipse, the labours of the moon;

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The race of men and beasts, the storm, the fire,

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Arcturus’ rainy Hyads, and the Bears:

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Why haste the winter’s suns to bathe themselves

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Beneath the wave, what stays its lingering nights.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Then, leaving things in heaven, and condescending to those on earth, you philosophize on minor points.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You say: “Tell us what are the causes of the fountains, and of the wind; what makes the hail and the showers; why the sea is salt, the rivers sweet; what account is to be given of clouds and storms, thunderbolts, and thunder and lightning.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You mean that if I do not know all this, you are entitled to say you know nothing about the origin of souls.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You wish to balance your ignorance on a single point by mine on many.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But do not you, who in page after page stir up what you call my smoke, understand that I can see your mists and whirlwinds?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You wish to be thought a man of extensive knowledge, and among the disciples of Calpurnius to enjoy a great reputation for wisdom, and therefore you raise up the whole physical world in front of me, as if Socrates had said in vain when he passed over to the study of Ethics: “What is above us is nothing to us.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: So then, if I cannot tell you why the ant, which is such a little creature, whose body is a mere point, has six feet, whereas an elephant with its vast bulk has only four to walk on; why serpents and snakes glide along on their chests and bellies; why the worm which is commonly called the millipede has such a swarming array of feet; I am prohibited from knowing anything about the origin of souls!

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You ask me what I know about souls, so that, when I make any statement about them, you may at once attack it.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: And if I say that the church’s doctrine is that God forms souls every day, and sends them into the bodies of those who are born, you will at once bring out the snares your master invented, and ask, Where is God’s justice if he grants souls to those who are born of adultery or incest?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Is he not an accessory to men’s sins, if he creates souls for the adulterers who make the bodies? as if, when you hear that seed corn had been stolen, you are to suppose the fault to lie in the nature of the corn, and not in the man who stole the wheat; and that therefore the earth had no business to nourish the seed in its bosom, because the hands of the sower who cast them in were unclean.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Hence comes also your mysterious question, Why do infants die? since it is because of their sins, as you hold, that they received bodies.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: There exists a treatise of Didymus addressed to you, in which he meets this inquiry of yours, with the answer, that they had not sinned much, and therefore it was enough punishment for them just to have touched their bodily prisons.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He, who was your master and mine also, when you asked this question, wrote at my request three books of comments on the prophet Hosea, and dedicated them to me.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: This shows what parts of his teaching we respectively accepted.
